summary: I currently live in Lexington, Kentucky, mainly because it's one of the most affordable places I've ever lived. I moved here from Miami as part of a long-term plan since Miami is slowly sinking under the ocean. Lexington has become my home where I garden for a living and help others garden too.

I grew up in Miami, Florida, specifically in a neighborhood called Opa-locka. Miami was my childhood home, but I chose to leave because of environmental concerns like the city sinking under the ocean. Miami is also a major hub for the Jamaican diaspora, which connects to my family's migration story.

Opa-locka is where I grew up, and it's a fascinating place because it was a planned city themed on the Arabian Nights stories, also known as One Thousand and One Nights. The city has lots of Arabian-style buildings, domes, and minarets, and I lived on Arabia Avenue. It was developed in the 1920s by Glenn Curtiss and architects from New York City to attract northerners to Florida.

The stories of One Thousand and One Nights inspired the design and theme of Opa-locka, Florida, where I grew up. Glenn Curtiss and New York architects used these Arabian Nights tales to create a fantastical planned community with streets and buildings reflecting the stories. It was a unique cultural influence on my childhood environment.

Glenn Curtiss was a famous aviator in the 1920s who played a key role in developing Opa-locka, Florida, as a planned city themed on One Thousand and One Nights. Although not widely remembered today, he was incredibly wealthy and collaborated with New York architects to build this unique Arabian-style community. His vision shaped the place where I spent my childhood.

My family is originally from Jamaica, and they moved to the United States in the late 1970s. I spent many summers visiting Jamaica, especially Kingston, where my grandparents lived. Jamaica's tropical environment and agricultural traditions from places like St. Elizabeth Parish influenced my upbringing and connection to gardening.

Opa-locka, where I grew up, is about twenty minutes from North Miami and the beach. This proximity shaped my childhood experiences, giving me access to the coast and nearby urban areas. North Miami was part of the broader region of my early life in Florida.

I had school trips to the Everglades growing up in Florida, although my family didn't take outdoor trips there. The Everglades is a unique park where nature is always reclaiming the land, and it stood out to me during my PhD studies in Tampa. It represents the wild, subtropical environment of South Florida that contrasts with urban life.

I completed my PhD at the University of South Florida in Tampa. During my time there, I learned a lot about Florida's natural environment, including the Everglades, which a friend described as a place nature will always take back. USF was an important part of my academic journey and understanding of Florida's ecology.

South Florida is where I grew up and where many Jamaicans, including my family, have settled. It has a subtropical climate with ecosystems similar to Jamaica's tropical environment. South Florida also represents a cultural hub for the Jamaican diaspora, connecting me to my heritage and community.

Kingston is where my grandparents lived, and I spent many summers there as a child. My grandmother maintained a large garden with fruit trees and animals, which left a lasting impression on me. Although the house is no longer there, the garden's fruit trees remain and are cherished by neighbors.

My grandmother was originally from St. Elizabeth Parish, known as the breadbasket of Jamaica because of its agricultural community. This background influenced her gardening skills and the way she maintained her garden in Kingston. It connects me to Jamaica's rural farming traditions despite growing up in urban Florida.

My grandfather was a man who loved reading and staying informed, often listening to the BBC for news and political commentary. This habit reflected his literacy and engagement with world affairs, which contrasted with my grandmother's lack of literacy. The BBC was part of his daily routine in Kingston.

My family is part of the Jamaican diaspora, having moved to the United States in the late 1970s. Many Jamaicans live outside the island now, especially in places like Miami, Orlando, New York City, England, and Canada. The diaspora experience shapes how we maintain connections to Jamaica despite economic challenges on the island.

England is one of the main destinations for the Jamaican diaspora, alongside Miami and other cities. Many Jamaicans have settled there, maintaining cultural ties and contributing to the broader diaspora network. It's part of the traditional pathways where Jamaicans have established communities abroad.

Orlando is another key city where many Jamaicans have settled, although Miami has a larger Jamaican population. It's part of the network of places in the U.S. where the Jamaican diaspora lives and maintains cultural ties. Orlando represents one of the traditional hubs for Jamaicans outside the island.

New York City is one of the major urban centers where many Jamaicans have settled as part of the diaspora. It is a traditional nodal point alongside Miami, Orlando, England, and Canada. The city represents an important cultural and economic hub for Jamaicans living abroad.

Canada, especially areas around Ottawa, is another significant destination for the Jamaican diaspora. Many Jamaicans have settled there, maintaining connections to their heritage while living abroad. It is part of the broader pattern of migration from Jamaica to English-speaking countries.

Ottawa is a key city in Canada where many Jamaicans have established communities as part of the diaspora. It represents one of the eastern Canadian hubs for Jamaican immigrants. This city is part of the traditional migration routes for Jamaicans seeking opportunities abroad.

I applied to Reed College because it seemed like a wild and wonderful place, with things like a dog watering spot on campus. It was exciting to see such a different college experience compared to what I knew growing up in Florida. Although I didn't attend, it was part of my exploration of schools outside my home state.

I ultimately chose to attend Syracuse University because of the Maxwell School and its strong international relations program. It was far from Florida, and I was drawn to the idea of experiencing seasons and snow for the first time. My first winter there was dreadful, and I had to learn how to dress for the cold, including buying coats and boots.

entity: Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs
summary: The Maxwell School at Syracuse University was a major reason I chose to attend there, as I was interested in international relations and diplomacy. I hoped to become an international lawyer or diplomat, and Maxwell seemed like the perfect place for that. Although my career path changed, Maxwell shaped my academic beginnings.

When I moved to Syracuse for college, I traveled with my mom on Greyhound Lines from Miami. It was quite an experience arriving without a proper winter coat and having to shop for one after getting there. That bus trip marked the beginning of my transition from Florida to upstate New York.

After arriving in Syracuse without a winter coat, I had to go to the Carousel Mall to buy one. My first coat was from a store like Forever 21 and was not very warm, but it was a start. The mall was an important place for me to adapt to the cold climate and prepare for my first winter.

My first winter coat in Syracuse was probably from Forever 21, and it was a puffy white coat that was not very warm or practical. I had to go through several coats before finding one that was good enough for the harsh winters. Forever 21 was part of my early attempts to adjust to the cold weather.

At Syracuse University, I lived in the dormitories on Mount Olympus, specifically in Flint dorm. Getting to the main campus involved going down about 144 stairs, which was quite a trek, especially in winter. Mount Olympus was my home base during my college years.
Nick Drake was not mentioned in my story, so I don't have a personal connection or experience to share about him.
Berkeley College of Music did not come up in my narrative, so I don't have any experiences or stories related to it.
Felicity, the TV series, was not part of my story or experiences, so I have no personal connection to it.
